coredata.dc:title: Alendronate-derived carbon dots for bioimaging and effective osteoblastic differentiation induction and promotion
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jiajia
Yuan, Song
Jiang, Yuxiang
Jia, Yinnong
Ji, Chunyu
Tan, Zhuomin
Shi, Wenquan
Peng, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) belong to zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, which are promising in various biomedical applications including imaging, biosensing and drug delivery. In this work, we have synthesized novel fluorescent CDs (Alen-CDs) derived from alendronate, an anti-osteoporosis drug, through a one-step hydrothermal method. The ability of Alen-CDs for fluorescent imaging was evaluated via in vitro cellular bioimaging. In addition, the capability of Alen-CDs to induce in vitro osteoblastic differentiation was investigated by alkaline phosphatase staining, activity test and alizarin red staining. As demonstrated, Alen-CDs was effective for in vitro fluorescent imaging without obvious toxicity. Also, Alen-CDs promoted the survival of rat bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells and stimulated osteoblastic differentiation. In conclusion, Alen-CDs are a new type of nanomaterial with good biocompatibility and fluorescent properties. Alen-CDs could be applied as a potential probe for bioimaging and a promising nanodrug for bone regeneration as well as the combination to be used as a theranostic agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Alendronate
Bioimaging
Osteoblastic differentiation
Biocompatibility

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Z. Peng and Y. Jia gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under the grant numbers 21807010 and 82160344, and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province under the grant numbers 202301AT070147 and 202001AU070141. Authors thank Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for sample testing service.

Z. Peng and Y. Jia gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under the grant numbers 21807010 and 82160344 , and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province under the grant numbers 202301AT070147 and 202001AU070141 . Authors thank Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for sample testing service.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological and structural characterizations of Alen-CDs: a) schematic illustration of the synthesis of Alen-CDs; b) TEM image, the upper right inset is the enlarged image of the specific particle, and the lower left inset is size distribution histogram of Alen-CDs; c) XRD pattern of Alen-CDs; d) Raman spectrum of Alen-CDs; e) The UV–vis absorption spectra of alendronate and Alen-CDs, the insets are photographs of Alen-CDs under daylight (left) and 405 nm handheld UV light (right); f) PL spectra under various wavelengths excitation of Alen-CDs.
a) The FT-IR spectra: b) full-scan XPS spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of c) C 1s, d) N 1s, e) O 1s, f) P 2p spectra of Alen-CDs.

The confocal fluorescent microscopy images of a-d) MDA-MB-435s cells and e-h) rBMSCs cells incubated with Alen-CDs for 4 h. a and e) images corresponding to bright field, cells were highlighted with red dashed circles; b and f) images corresponding to green fluorescence by Alen-CDs (λex = 458 nm); c and g) images corresponding to blue fluorescence stained by DAPI (λex = 405 nm); d and h) images corresponding to the merge of green and blue fluorescent images. The concentrations of Alen-CDs are 100 μg/mL and the scale bar is 25 μm.

The alkaline phosphatase (ALP) staining figures of rBMSCs cultured with different mediums taken on day-7 (a1-d1), day-14 (a2-d2) and day-21 (a3-d3), respectively. NM (a1-a3) denotes cells cultured in normal medium without Alen-CDs; NM + Alen-CDs (b1-b3) denotes cells cultured in normal medium with Alen-CDs; OM (c1-c3) denotes cells cultured in osteogenic differentiation medium without Alen-CDs; OM + Alen-CDs (d1-d3) denotes cells cultured in osteogenic differentiation medium with Alen-CDs. The concentrations of Alen-CDs are 50 μg/mL and the scale bar is 100 μm.

Comparative results of ALP activities of rBMSCs cultured in NM, NM + Alen-CDs, OM, and OM + Alen-CDs groups at day 7, 14 and 21, respectively. NM denotes normal medium without Alen-CDs; NM + Alen-CDs denotes normal medium with Alen-CDs; OM denotes osteogenic differentiation medium without Alen-CDs and OM + Alen-CDs denotes osteogenic differentiation medium with Alen-CDs. The concentrations of Alen-CDs were set at 50 μg/mL.

Figures showing the calcium deposition of rBMSCs evaluated by alizarin red S (ARS) staining taken on day-7 (a1-d1), day-14 (a2-d2) and day-21 (a3-d3), respectively. NM (a1-a3) denotes cells cultured in normal medium without Alen-CDs; NM + Alen-CDs (b1-b3) denotes cells cultured in normal medium with Alen-CDs; OM (c1-c3) denotes cells cultured in osteogenic differentiation medium without Alen-CDs; OM + Alen-CDs (d1-d3) denotes cells cultured in osteogenic differentiation medium with Alen-CDs. The concentrations of Alen-CDs are 50 μg/mL and the scale bar is 100 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Alendronate-derived carbon dots for bioimaging and effective osteoblastic differentiation induction and promotion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology, School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology, School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, People'’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Provincial Key Laboratory of Pharmacology for Natural Products, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Kunming Medical University, Kunming 650500, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Provincial Key Laboratory of Pharmacology for Natural Products

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Provincial Key Laboratory of Pharmacology for Natural Products, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Kunming Medical University, Kunming 650500, People'’s Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) belong to zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, which are promising in various biomedical applications including imaging, biosensing and drug delivery. In this work, we have synthesized novel fluorescent CDs (Alen-CDs) derived from alendronate, an anti-osteoporosis drug, through a one-step hydrothermal method. The ability of Alen-CDs for fluorescent imaging was evaluated via in vitro cellular bioimaging. In addition, the capability of Alen-CDs to induce in vitro osteoblastic differentiation was investigated by alkaline phosphatase staining, activity test and alizarin red staining. As demonstrated, Alen-CDs was effective for in vitro fluorescent imaging without obvious toxicity. Also, Alen-CDs promoted the survival of rat bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells and stimulated osteoblastic differentiation. In conclusion, Alen-CDs are a new type of nanomaterial with good biocompatibility and fluorescent properties. Alen-CDs could be applied as a potential probe for bioimaging and a promising nanodrug for bone regeneration as well as the combination to be used as a theranostic agent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, including graphene quantum dots, carbon quantum dots, carbon nanodots and polymer dots [1], which could be prepared via “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches [2]. CDs exhibit superior advantages including unique photoluminescence (PL) properties, excellent biocompatibility and fluorescent stability [3], thus, they have been applied broadly for various biological applications, such as bioimaging, drug delivery [4,5] and biosensing [6]. Among the various favorable properties of CDs, one intriguing property has attracted significant attention that certain intrinsic characteristics of carbon precursors (the original material) could be inherited and preserved by the newly synthesized CDs [7,8]. This unique phenomenon provided materials scientists and chemists strong motivation to modify carbon precursors such as dyes and drugs with targeting or treatment effects. In newly synthesized C-dots, improved bioavailability, PL and stability would be achieved with the remaining of unique properties from the “parent” molecule. For instance, Xie and co-workers prepared near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent CDs derived from cyanine dye, CyOH, which not only improved the biocompatibility and hydrophilicity of the precursor but also retained the NIR emissive capability [9]. In a different study, Li et al. synthesized porphyrin-derived photodynamic CDs, in which the limitations of porphyrin such as poor water solubility and biocompatibility were ameliorated without diminishing the photodynamic activity [10]. Our group also reported the enhancement of the PL and photothermal conversion efficiency of indocyanine green via simple carbonization and demonstrated their in vitro and in vivo applications [11].

As demonstrated, various carbon precursors have been selected and the preparation of different CDs with much enhanced properties for biological applications has been realized [12,13]. Inspired by these studies, this strategy has also been utilized by scientists to rejuvenate traditional Chinese medicine [14]. For instance, Curcuma longa leaves and red Korean ginseng roots were used as raw materials to prepare CDs, which demonstrated excellent antibacterial and antioxidant activities [15,16]. CDs synthesized by Schizonepetae Herba Carbonisata and Pollen Typhae Carbonisata exerted hemostatic effects [17,18]. And CDs sourced from Lonicerae japonicae Flos and Puerariae lobatae Radix displayed favorable anti-inflammatory and anti-gout effects [19,20]. In addition to CDs sourced from traditional Chinese medicine, chemical drugs were also used to prepare CDs with the diverse medicinal properties. For example, Xu et al. synthesized aspirin-derived CDs that presented excellent anti-inflammatory and fluorescent biomarker dual-functions. Moreover, Liu et al. prepared CDs from metronidazole and achieved selective antibacterial activity against Porphyromonas gingivalis [21,22]. Furthermore, folic acid and hyaluronic acid are known for their targeting specificity toward folate receptor and CD44 overexpressed cancers cells. To exploit the phenomena, scientists have demonstrated that folic acid and hyaluronic acid-derived CDs were very effective in labeling and imaging these cancer cells [23,24].

As we entered into an aging society, the incidence of age-related osteoporotic fractures climbed up, which has attracted much attention [25,26]. Osteoporosis as a growing public health problem affects 319million population, especially adults over age 50 [27]. In clinic, osteoporotic fractures are the most common and detrimental complications of osteoporosis impairing the quality of life of patients. Bone homeostasis is synergistically regulated by osteoclastic bone-resorption and osteoblastic bone-formation, and the disruption of this equilibrium will lead to osteoporosis [28,29]. At present, the prevention for osteoporotic fractures is mainly via the administration of drugs that could reduce fracture risk, such as bisphosphonates, teriparatide and raloxifene. Bisphosphonates are among the most effective drugs for bone and calcium metabolism-related diseases. They generally exhibit high affinity for calcium ions and demonstrate excellent anti-osteoporosis effects by preventing the formation of osteoclasts and the apoptosis of osteoblasts [30]. Alendronate is a second-generation bisphosphonate drug, of which the side chain was inserted by an amino group. Compared to the first-generation bisphosphonates, its ability to resist bone resorption is enhanced remarkably and widely applied in clinic for the prevention and treatment of osteoporosis in postmenopausal women and osteoporosis in men [31,32]. In such context, we aimed to prepare alendronate-derived CDs (Alen-CDs) via the carbonization of alendronate that not only inherit the anti-osteoporosis capability (i.e., bone targeting, proliferation and osteoblast differentiation promoting) but also obtain much-enhanced chemical, PL and biological properties.

Herein, we reported our latest finding in developing a simple carbonization strategy for the preparation of nanosized Alen-CDs and their applications as bioimaging and anti-osteoporosis agents. Specifically, Alen-CDs were fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal treatment from bisphosphonate drug alendronate integrated with typical characteristics of CDs. As expected, Alen-CDs inherited the anti-osteoporosis capability of alendronate with much improved water dispersion ability and PL properties. Cell viability assay demonstrated that Alen-CDs were easily uptaken by MDA-MB-435s and rat bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (rBMSCs) with very low cytotoxicity, thus to realize the effective cellular imaging. Most importantly, as illustrated by alkaline phosphatase (ALP) staining and alizarin red S (ARS) staining calcium deposition studies, Alen-CDs effectively promoted osteogenic differentiation of rBMSCs in both normal and osteogenic induction mediums (NM and OM). The study demonstrated that the carbonization of traditional drug molecules (i.e., alendronate) to form CDs is a convenient and applicable strategy to improve unfavorable properties with desirable characteristics.

Alendronate sodium trihydrate (98 %) was purchased from Energy Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Low-glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium and penicillin–streptomycin were purchased from Gibco Co., Ltd. (Carlsbad, CA, USA). OriCell® rat bone marrow mesenchymal stem cell osteogenic differentiation medium was purchased from Cyagen Biosciences (Guangzhou, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from life technologies (Grand Island, NY, USA). DAPI staining solution was purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 4 % paraformaldehyde was purchased from Biosharp Biotechnology (Hefei, China). Cell lysis buffer for Western and IP without inhibitors, BCIP/NBT Alkaline Phosphatase Color Development Kit, ARS Staining Kit for Osteogenesis, Alkaline Phosphatase Assay Kit and enhanced Bicinchoninic Acid (BCA) protein assay kits were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The reagents were used as received without further purification, unless otherwise noted. Deionized (DI) water used in all experiments was made from a Master Touch-S laboratory ultrapure water instrument (Master Touch, Shanghai, China).

Alen-CDs were synthesized using alendronate as the raw material via a one-step hydrothermal carbonization. Briefly, alendronate was dissolved in DI water, then the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to r.t., the resulted reaction was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 15 min and then filtered by a 0.22 μm filter to remove any bulky particles. The resulted dispersion was vacuum dried to yield black colloid as the final product.

The morphology of Alen-CDs was measured by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on an TTRIII X-ray diffractometer (DX-2700BH, Haoyuan Instrument Co., Ltd., Dandong, Liaoning, China) with a wavelength (λ) of 0.15406 nm. The Raman spectra were obtained on a Micro Confocal Raman Spectrometer (inVia, Renishaw, New Mills, Gloucestershire, UK) with excitation wavelength set at 785 nm. The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of Alen-CDs were measured by an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan), and fluorescence spectrometer (F97 Pro Shanghai Prism Technology Co., Ltd., China) in a 1 × 1 cm quartz cuvette, respectively. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by a FTIR spectrometer (Middle Age Walker, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The XPS spectra were obtained on an X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (K-Alpha+, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) and the X-ray source is monochromatic Al Kα with a hυ = 1486.6 eV photo energy operated at 6 mA × 12 kV. The optical absorbance was measured by a microplate reader (FC, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Cell fluorescence images were conducted on an inverted fluorescence microscope (NiU, Nikon, Japan). ALP and ARS staining images were conducted on an inverted fluorescence microscope (CKX31, Olympus, Japan).

The rBMSCs were isolated from femurs of SD rats (80–100 g). Briefly, rBMSCs were flushed out using syringe and plated in T-75 cell culture flask (Corning, USA). The cells were cultured in Low-glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium supplemented with 15 % FBS, 1 % penicillin and streptomycin in an incubator with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. The medium was changed every 3 days. The third passage of rBMSCs was prepared for the following experiments. The human breast cancer cells MDA-MB-435s were cultured in the Low-glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium with 10 % FBS, 1 % penicillin and streptomycin.

The cytotoxicity of Alen-CDs was evaluated using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay with rBMSCs and MDA-MB-435s cells as model. Briefly, rBMSCs and MDA-MB-435s cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 6.3 × 103 cells per well for 24 h with 80 %–90 % confluency. Dispersions of Alen-CDs with gradient concentrations (31.25, 62.5, 125, 250 and 500 μg/mL) were added into different wells and incubated for 48 h in corresponding media, and the control group was set without the presence of Alen-CDs. MTT solution (20 μL, 5 mg/mL) were then added to each well and incubated for another 4 h. Then, the supernatant was removed and 100 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the insoluble formazan crystals. The resulted mixture was shaken for 15 s at r.t. and then the optical absorbance was measured at 570 and 630 nm by a microplate reader (FC, Thermo, USA) to evaluate the cell viability.

The MDA-MB-435s and rBMSCs were planted in a glass bottom microwell dish at a density of 2 × 105 cells per well and incubated for 24 h with 80 %–90 % confluency. Alen-CDs (200 μL, 1 mg/mL) were added into each well and incubated for 4 h. Then, the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) for three times to discard the excess Alen-CDs, after which the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution for 15 min. To stain with DAPI, the fixative was aspirated and the cells were washed three more times with PBS, then a small amount of DAPI was dropped into wells and shaken evenly. The fluorescence images were obtained on a two-photon laser confocal microscope (TCS SP8 DIVE, Leika, Germany).

The experiment was carried out to assess the osteogenic differentiation behavior of rBMSCs in the presence of Alen-CDs. Specifically, rBMSCs were cultured at a density of 2 × 105 in T25 culture flasks and 5 × 104 cells per well in 12-well plates for 24 h, then 50 μg/mL of Alen-CDs were added to incubate the cells, and the medium was changed to osteogenic differentiation medium (OM), the control group was cultured with normal medium (NM). The rBMSCs in different media w/o Alen-CDs were designated as four experimental groups: OM; OM+Alen-CDs; NM; and NM+Alen-CDs. After culturing for 7, 14 and 21 days, the osteoblast differentiation was carefully evaluated respectively.

For ALP staining, rBMSCs were cultured in 12-well plates for 7, 14 and 21 days, respectively. At the day of ALP staining, rBMSCs were washed with PBS and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde at r.t. for 30 min. Subsequently, rBMSCs were incubated with ALP staining solution for 2 h until the color became very light. After removing the staining solution, the cells were washed with PBS to terminate the reaction. Finally, the staining images were obtained on an inverted fluorescence microscope (CKX31, Olympus, Japan). The ARS staining was performed to analyze the mineralized matrix of rBMSCs after osteogenic induction. The procedures for ARS staining were same as above described except ARS staining solution was used instead of ALP staining solution.

To perform the ALP and BCA activity assays, rBMSCs were cultured for 7, 14 and 21 days, respectively, and then digested with cell lysis buffer for 1 min. The lysis solution with rBMSCs was collected and centrifuged, then the supernatant was diluted to prepare the sample for next step. Finally, the ALP activity and total protein contents were analyzed by Alkaline Phosphatase Assay Kit and BCA kit following the manufacturers' instructions. The relative ALP activities of different samples were normalized according to the protein contents of the cells.

The data were expressed as means ± SD. All experiments were repeated at least three times unless otherwise indicated. The statistical significance of the data was compared by student's t-test. An analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to analyze the differences among groups. The statistical significance for all experiments was set at P < 0.05.

To elucidate the morphological, structural, optical and chemical properties of Alen-CDs, various characterizations were applied. Firstly, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphologies of Alen-CDs. The TEM image showed that Alen-CDs were well-dispersed spherical particles with no apparent aggregation (Fig. 1 b). According to the size distribution histogram, these particles exhibited diameters ranging from 4.02 to 6.35 nm, with an average diameter of 5.25 nm (Fig. 1 b, lower left inset). A crystalline core with lattice spacing of ~0.21 nm was observed in the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1 b, upper right inset), which could be attributed to the (100) diffraction facets of graphite structure, indicating the successful carbonization and graphitization of the carbon precursor [33]. The XRD pattern of Alen-CDs was further analyzed, demonstrating a broad peak centered at 23.11° (Fig. 1 c). The peak was frequently observed for CDs and generally regarded as a solid evidence for the successful formation of CDs [34]. Following TEM and XRD analysis, the Raman spectrum of Alen-CDs was also obtained, in which sharp and intense peak for G-band and feeble peak for D-band at 1573 and 1392 cm−1 were observed, respectively, indicating the successful carbonization of alendronate (Fig. 1 d). The low ratio of I D/I G (0.59) for Alen-CDs also suggested the existence of highly ordered sp2 clusters with few structure defects [35].

Besides the morphological and structural properties, we then carefully evaluated the optical features of Alen-CDs. As demonstrated in the UV–Vis spectrum, the peaks at 230 and 265 nm were ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO, respectively, which were drastically different from those of alendronate (Fig. 1 e). The aqueous dispersion of Alen-CDs was transparent golden yellow under ambient light, and transited to bright blue upon excited by a 405 nm handheld UV lamp (Fig. 1 e, inset). Similar to other CDs reported, Alen-CDs demonstrated the typical excitation-wavelength-dependent emission behavior (Fig. 1 f), in which the emissions shifted from 410 to 520 nm as the excitation wavelengths increased from 260 to 460 nm (Fig. 1 f, inset). As alendronate itself was not emission active, thus the excellent PL emissions observed here further supported the successful synthesis of Alen-CDs. To our delight, the fluorescence of Alen-CDs was very stable, which exhibited strong resistance to high ionic strength (Fig. S2) and photobleaching (Fig. S3). In addition, after being stored at room temperature for five days, the fluorescence performance of Alen-CDs, including the center position of emission wavelength and PL intensity (Fig. S4), remained essential the same with no obvious change, further highlighting the excellent PL stability of Alen-CDs.

In addition to FTIR, Alen-CDs were further characterized by XPS. The peaks of Alen-CDs at 533.13, 402.65, 286.24 and 135.06 eV indicated the presence of oxygen, nitrogen, carbon and phosphorous, respectively (Fig. 2 b). According to the content ratio calculated from the XPS spectra, the ratio of C: N: O: P was 0.44: 0.07: 0.37: 0.12, suggesting high amount of carbon and oxygen was introduced during the formation of Alen-CDs. The deconvoluted C 1s spectra of Alen-CDs demonstrated four peaks at 286.7, 286.2, 285.5 and 284.8 eV, corresponding to CN, CO, CN and CC, respectively (Fig. 2 c). The deconvoluted N 1s spectra demonstrated two peaks at 401.1 and 400.4 eV, which were ascribed to graphitic and amino nitrogen, respectively (Fig. 2 d). In the deconvoluted O 1s spectra of the Alen-CDs, there were four peaks at 532.4, 531.8, 531.1 and 530.5 eV, corresponding to PO, CO, PO and CO, respectively [37] (Fig. 2 e), demonstrating the rich oxygen-containing function groups presence of Alen-CDs. The deconvoluted P 2p spectra of Alen-CDs revealed two peaks at 134.15 and 133.05 eV, which could be devoted to PO and PO (Fig. 2 f).

With the successful morphological, structural, optical and chemical characterization, the potential applications of Alen-CDs for bioimaging and bone-formation promoting agent were also demonstrated. First, the cytotoxicity of Alen-CDs was evaluated using rBMSCs and MDA-MB-435s cells as the cell model. To our delight, Alen-CDs demonstrated high degree of biocompatibility as supported by the MTT assay results. Specifically, only slight decrease of cell viability for both cells were observed as the concentrations of Alen-CDs gradually increased from 0 to 500 μg/mL (Fig. S5). Furthermore, the cell viability was still above 80 % even when the concentration of Alen-CDs was increased to 500 μg/mL, demonstrating the low cytotoxicity of Alen-CDs.

Subsequently, the feasibility of Alen-CDs as bioimaging agent was first demonstrated using MDA-MB-435s and rBMSCs cells as the in vitro cell model. Based on the results of cytotoxicity experiments, an appropriate concentration of Alen-CDs at 100 μg/mL was selected to perform cellular imaging. Particularly, under 458 nm excitation light, a remarkable fluorescence inside the MDA-MB-435s could be clearly observed in the confocal fluorescent microscopy image, which could be attributed to the fluorescence of Alen-CDs (Fig. 3 b ). To identify the accurate location of Alen-CDs inside the cells, the cell nuclei were stained with DAPI (Fig. 3 c) and merged with the green fluorescence of Alen-CDs (Fig. 3 b); the merged image (Fig. 3 d) showed that Alen-CDs were mainly distributed inside the nucleus, and only a slight portion was present in cytoplasm, highlighting the excellent cell-permeability of Alen-CDs. Similar results could be observed in the fluorescence images of rBMSCs cells (Fig. 3 f-h) and all the cells treated with Alen-CDs maintained their normal morphology (Fig. 3 a, e) with no obvious green fluorescence weakening after long period of continuous exposure, demonstrating the high cellular photostability of Alen-CDs.

Generally speaking, the formation of new bones is mainly the result of the bioactivities of osteoblasts, including bone matrix formation, proliferation, differentiation, extracellular matrix deposition, mineralization and maturation [38,39]. ALP is a matrix protein secreted by osteoblasts to facilitate bone mineralization by providing mineral molecules, thus it is an important biomarker for osteoblast activity during early bone formation [40,41]. To evaluate the effectiveness of Alen-CDs on inducing osteoblastic differentiation, the osteoblastic differentiation activity of rBMSCs in the presence or absence of Alen-CDs was monitored and evaluated based on respective ALP staining and activity.

In general, the more dark-blue precipitations observed in the ALP staining, the more ALP was presented. From the experiments, it was concluded that the osteoblast activities of rBMSCs were very weak in the first 7 days in all mediums with or without Alen-CDs (Fig. 4 , a1-d1) compared to that of 14 and 21 days (Fig. 4, a2-d2 and a3-d3), indicating the mature of osteoblastic differentiation required a certain amount of time even in the presence of an inducer (i.e., Alen-CDs) [42]. As expected, the osteoblast activities of rBMSCs were higher in the control of OM only (Fig. 4, c1-c3) than that of NM only (Fig. 4, a1-a3) in all the time intervals (i.e., Day 7, 14 and 21) tested, as OM is a specially configured medium, in which additives such as β-glycerophosphate and glutamine that are beneficial for inducing osteogenic differentiation were added [43].

To our delight, the Alen-CDs demonstrated obvious positive effects on the osteogenic differentiation of rBMSCs at all time intervals (i.e., Day 7, 14 and 21), as indicated by the significantly increased ALP positive cells in either NM (Fig. 4, b1-b3 vs. a1-a3) or OM (Fig. 4, d1-d3 vs. c1-c3) mediums. To investigate whether there is a synergistic effect on rBMSCs osteogenic differentiation between the OM and Alen-CDs, the osteoblast activities in NM with Alen-CDs (Fig. 4, b1-b3) were carefully compared with those of OM with Alen-CDs (Fig. 4, d1-d3). Surprisingly, the ALP positive cells in OM + Alen-CDs group were less than those in NM + Alen-CDs group at day-14, and we cautiously attributed this counterintuitive result to the fact that the synergistic induction and promotion of OM and Alen-CDs on rBMSCs had led the cells differentiate and age faster and at an earlier stage than those in NM + Alen-CDs group. As such, at day-14, rBMSCs in OM + Alen-CDs group have already peaked through the differentiation period and went into maturity and osteogenesis [44], generating less ALP and exhibiting less and lighter staining images. On the contrary, the rBMSCs in NM + Alen-CDs group aged slower, and thus could generate more ALP and darker images after ALP staining at day-14 (Fig. 4, d2 vs. b2).

Compared to ALP staining, ALP activity assay could reflect the effects of Alen-CDs more accurately since it was presented in the form of cell activity values. To our delight, the ALP activity assay demonstrated similar trends as we observed in the ALP staining experiments, that is the ALP activities at day-7 were very weak compared to these at 14 and 21 days in all mediums with or without Alen-CDs (Fig. 5 ). Specifically, the ALP activity at day-14 was much higher than that of the other two time intervals (day-7 and -21), indicating that the richest ALP expression of rBMSCs was around day-14 when the bone matrix gone through a whole mineralization process [45]: 7 days were too early for the rBMSCs to secrete ALP and 21 days were too mature. There results corresponded well with what we observed in the ALP staining experiment.

It's worth noting, Alen-CDs had almost no effects on osteogenic induction compared to the control groups at day-7, which was different from the observations in ALP staining experiment. However, 40.7 % and 57.4 % increases in the ALP activity was observed at day-14 for the cells cultured in NM and OM mediums, respectively. Similar to what observed in the ALP staining experiment, as the time extended to 21 days, the bone matrix demonstrated an increase in calcium nodules and decrease in ALP activity in both the presence and absence of Alen-CDs due to maturation [46,47]. Another reason that might explain the decrease in ALP activity at day-21 was the potential aging and dying of rBMSCs over time, which could reduce the overall ALP expression. Collectively consider the results from ALP staining and activity experiments, it is safe to conclude that more ALP were produced in the presence of Alen-CDs, highlighting that Alen-CDs could indeed induce and promote the osteogenic differentiation of rBMSCs.

In addition to ALP activity, osteogenic differentiation could also be evaluated by the degree of calcium deposits formation during bone matrix mineralization and maturation [48], which could be facilely realized via ARS staining by monitoring the orange-red complex formed due to the chelation of ARS with calcium salts in osteocalcin [49,50]. As such, in addition to ALP staining and activity, we also carried out ARS staining experiment to evaluate the effects of Alen-CDs on osteogenic differentiation. As visualized in the ARS staining images (Fig. 6 ), calcium deposition was observed in all groups, and the deposits on day-14 and -21 were significantly higher than that on day-7, demonstrating a positive correlation to time. This observation could be attributed to that calcium deposition was more exuberant in the late stage of osteogenic differentiation [51,52]. Similar to ALP staining, the calcium deposition of rBMSCs was higher in the control of OM only (Fig. 6, c1-c3) than that of NM only (Fig. 6, a1-a3) in all the time intervals (i.e., Day-7, −14 and − 21) monitored, with the ones at day 14 were most obvious.

To our delight, Alen-CDs again demonstrated obvious positive effects on the osteogenic differentiation of rBMSCs at all time intervals (i.e., Day-7, −14 and − 21), as indicated by the significantly increased calcium depositions in either NM (Fig. 6, b1-b3 vs. a1-a3) or OM (Fig. 6, d1-d3 vs. c1-c3) mediums. In short, the groups with Alen-CDs all exhibited higher density of calcified nodules when compared to the control groups. Notably, there were more calcium depositions at day-14 than at day-21 (Fig. 6, a2-d2 vs. a3-d3); and we attributed this phenomenon to the aging and dying of rBMSCs from day-14 to −21, which was similar to that observed in the previous ALP staining and activity experiments. These studies again highlighted the remarkable potential of Alen-CDs as efficient agents in inducing and promoting osteoblast differentiation.

In this study, we successfully fabricated bone-targeting Alen-CDs based on alendronate and demonstrated their photostability, biocompatibility and osteoblastic differentiation capability. Green fluorescent Alen-CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity toward MDA-MB-435s and rBMSCs, and were able to penetrate cell and nucleus membranes, emitting fluorescence in both nucleus and cytoplasm. Moreover, the results of in vitro osteoblastic differentiation assays showed Alen-CDs indeed promoted rBMSCs generating more ALP and calcium deposition, suggesting the ability to stimulate osteoblastic differentiation. In conclusion, the Alen-CDs nanomaterial was an excellent candidate for promoting osteoblastic differentiation, and demonstrated potential applications in bioimaging and bone tissue regeneration. In future, we would further evaluate the potential to apply Alen-CDs in osteoporosis prevention and treatment. In addition, the monitoring ability of Alen-CDs during treatment would be investigated as a theranostic agent.

Jiajia Wu: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Song Yuan: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yuxiang Jiang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yinnong Jia: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Chunyu Ji: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Zhuomin Tan: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Wenquan Shi: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Zhili Peng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Z. Peng and Y. Jia gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under the grant numbers 21807010 and 82160344, and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province under the grant numbers 202301AT070147 and 202001AU070141. Authors thank Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for sample testing service.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110571.
